content.text: The Azure CLI must authenticate to Azure before it can manage resources. There are four main authentication methods, each suited to different scenarios: interactive login for developers, service principal for automation with secrets, managed identity for workloads running inside Azure, and workload identity federation for passwordless CI/CD. Choosing the right method prevents secrets from leaking into logs and avoids MFA prompts in automated pipelines.

attrs.alt: Azure CLI authentication methods: interactive az login, service principal with secret, managed identity (no credentials), and OIDC workload identity federation — with credential precedence chain

content.text: Interactive login opens a browser for you to sign in with your Microsoft account. It supports MFA and Conditional Access policies. The token is cached in ~/.azure/ and reused for subsequent commands until it expires (typically 1 hour for access tokens, 90 days for refresh tokens).

# Standard interactive login (opens browser):
az login

# If the browser does not open automatically (headless server):
az login --use-device-code
# Outputs: "To sign in, use a web browser to open https://microsoft.com/devicelogin
#           and enter the code ABCD1234"
# Open that URL on any browser/device with your account

# Login to a specific tenant:
az login --tenant contoso.onmicrosoft.com
# or by tenant ID:
az login --tenant 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000

# After login, show the active account:
az account show -o table

# List all subscriptions your account can access:
az account list -o table

# Switch the active subscription:
az account set --subscription "sub-production"
# or by ID:
az account set --subscription "00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000"
The token cache at ~/.azure/ contains sensitive credentials. On shared machines, always run 'az logout' when finished. On personal machines, ensure the home directory is encrypted.

# Sign out the current account:
az logout

# Sign out a specific account:
az logout --username alice@contoso.com

# Clear ALL cached credentials (nuclear option):
az account clear
# This removes the token cache but not the config file

# Check which accounts are cached:
az account list -o table

A service principal is a non-human identity in Azure AD used for automated processes. You create an app registration, generate a client secret or certificate, then authenticate the CLI with those credentials. This is the standard approach for CI/CD pipelines on runners that do not support managed identity.

# Create a service principal with Contributor role on a subscription:
az ad sp create-for-rbac \
  --name sp-my-pipeline \
  --role Contributor \
  --scopes /subscriptions/<sub-id> \
  --output json

# Output (save these values securely):
# {
#   "appId": "aaaaaaaa-...",       ← CLIENT_ID
#   "displayName": "sp-my-pipeline",
#   "password": "xxxxx...",        ← CLIENT_SECRET (only shown once)
#   "tenant": "bbbbbbbb-..."       ← TENANT_ID
# }

# Create with a specific role scope (least privilege):
az ad sp create-for-rbac \
  --name sp-deploy-rg-prod \
  --role Contributor \
  --scopes /subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/rg-prod \
  --json-auth     # Outputs the JSON in the format expected by azure/login GitHub Action

# Login with client secret:
az login \
  --service-principal \
  --username "$APP_ID" \
  --password "$CLIENT_SECRET" \
  --tenant "$TENANT_ID"

# Login with a certificate (more secure — no secret in env var):
az login \
  --service-principal \
  --username "$APP_ID" \
  --certificate /path/to/cert.pem \
  --tenant "$TENANT_ID"

# Alternative: set environment variables and the CLI auto-authenticates:
export AZURE_CLIENT_ID="$APP_ID"
export AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET="$CLIENT_SECRET"
export AZURE_TENANT_ID="$TENANT_ID"
# Now run az commands without az login:
az account show

# List current credentials on a service principal:
az ad app credential list --id "$APP_ID" -o table

# Add a new secret (rotate before the old one expires):
az ad app credential reset \
  --id "$APP_ID" \
  --append \
  --years 1 \
  --display-name "rotation-$(date +%Y%m)"
# Save the new "password" output securely

# Delete the old secret after updating pipelines:
az ad app credential delete \
  --id "$APP_ID" \
  --key-id "<old-key-id>"

# Check when credentials expire:
az ad app credential list \
  --id "$APP_ID" \
  --query "[].{Display:displayName, EndDate:endDateTime}" \
  -o table

Managed identities are identities managed entirely by Azure — no passwords or secrets to store, rotate, or protect. They are only available to resources running inside Azure (VMs, App Service, AKS pods, Azure Functions, Container Instances, etc.).
Tied to the resource; deleted when resource is deleted
Can be assigned to multiple resources
Assign roles to the resource itself
Assign roles once; all attached resources inherit them
Multiple VMs sharing the same identity; microservices

# Enable system-assigned identity on a VM:
az vm identity assign \
  --name vm-prod-01 \
  --resource-group rg-prod

# Get the principal ID to assign RBAC roles:
PRINCIPAL_ID=$(az vm show \
  --name vm-prod-01 \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --query identity.principalId -o tsv)

# Grant the VM's identity Storage Blob Data Reader:
az role assignment create \
  --role "Storage Blob Data Reader" \
  --assignee-object-id "$PRINCIPAL_ID" \
  --assignee-principal-type ServicePrincipal \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/rg-prod/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/stprod001"

# On the VM, authenticate the CLI with the managed identity:
az login --identity

# Verify:
az account show  # shows the VM's managed identity as the signed-in principal

# Create a user-assigned managed identity:
az identity create \
  --name mi-shared-deployer \
  --resource-group rg-identities \
  --location eastus

# Assign it to a VM:
IDENTITY_ID=$(az identity show \
  --name mi-shared-deployer \
  --resource-group rg-identities \
  --query id -o tsv)

az vm identity assign \
  --name vm-prod-01 \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --identities "$IDENTITY_ID"

# On the VM, login with a user-assigned identity:
CLIENT_ID=$(az identity show \
  --name mi-shared-deployer \
  --resource-group rg-identities \
  --query clientId -o tsv)

az login --identity --username "$CLIENT_ID"

Workload identity federation lets external identity providers (GitHub Actions, Kubernetes, GitLab CI) authenticate to Azure without a client secret. The external system issues a JWT; Azure validates it against a trusted issuer configured on the app registration. This eliminates secrets entirely from CI/CD pipelines.

# 1. Create an app registration:
APP_ID=$(az ad app create --display-name sp-github-actions --query appId -o tsv)

# 2. Create a service principal for the app:
az ad sp create --id "$APP_ID"

# 3. Add a federated credential trusting GitHub Actions for a specific repo and branch:
az ad app federated-credential create \
  --id "$APP_ID" \
  --parameters '{
    "name": "github-main",
    "issuer": "https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com",
    "subject": "repo:myorg/myrepo:ref:refs/heads/main",
    "audiences": ["api://AzureADTokenExchange"]
  }'

# 4. Assign the app Contributor role on the target scope:
az role assignment create \
  --role Contributor \
  --assignee "$APP_ID" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/rg-prod"

echo "CLIENT_ID: $APP_ID"
echo "TENANT_ID: $(az account show --query tenantId -o tsv)"
echo "SUBSCRIPTION_ID: $(az account show --query id -o tsv)"

# .github/workflows/deploy.yml
name: Deploy

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

permissions:
  id-token: write    # required for OIDC
  contents: read

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: azure/login@v2
        with:
          client-id: ${{ vars.AZURE_CLIENT_ID }}
          tenant-id: ${{ vars.AZURE_TENANT_ID }}
          subscription-id: ${{ vars.AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID }}
          # No client-secret! OIDC token is issued by GitHub automatically.

      - run: az account show -o table

      - name: Deploy Bicep
        run: |
          az deployment group create \
            --resource-group rg-prod \
            --template-file main.bicep

# Show all cached accounts:
az account list -o table

# Show the currently active account:
az account show -o table

# Switch active subscription:
az account set --subscription "sub-production"

# Switch active tenant (requires login for that tenant):
az login --tenant "partner.onmicrosoft.com"

# Use a specific subscription for a single command without switching:
az vm list --subscription "sub-dev" -o table

# Show all tenants your account can access:
az account tenant list -o table

When you run 'az login', the CLI stores access tokens and refresh tokens in the ~/.azure directory. Understanding this cache helps with troubleshooting authentication failures and managing credentials on shared machines.

# ~/.azure directory contents after az login:
~/.azure/
  accessTokens.json   ← deprecated; modern CLI uses msal_token_cache.bin
  msal_token_cache.bin   ← MSAL token cache (encrypted on Windows via DPAPI)
  config                 ← CLI configuration (defaults, output format)
  telemetry/             ← telemetry data (sent to Microsoft unless disabled)

# Check when your token expires:
az account get-access-token --query "expiresOn" -o tsv
# Output: 2024-07-01 14:32:00.000000

# Get the raw access token (e.g., to pass to a REST API call):
TOKEN=$(az account get-access-token --query accessToken -o tsv)
curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions?api-version=2022-12-01" \
  | python3 -m json.tool

# Get a token scoped to a specific resource (not ARM):
az account get-access-token \
  --resource "https://graph.microsoft.com" \
  --query accessToken -o tsv

# The CLI sends usage telemetry to Microsoft by default.
# Disable it (persists in ~/.azure/config):
az configure --set core.collect_telemetry=no

# Or via environment variable (useful in CI):
export AZURE_CORE_COLLECT_TELEMETRY=no

# Verify:
az configure --list-defaults | grep telemetry

Engineers managing multiple Azure AD tenants (e.g., CSP partners, consultants with guest access to customer tenants) need to switch tenant context frequently. The CLI supports multiple concurrent login sessions.

# Login to multiple tenants in the same CLI session:
az login --tenant contoso.onmicrosoft.com   # first tenant
az login --tenant fabrikam.onmicrosoft.com  # second tenant (adds to cache)

# List all cached accounts across all tenants:
az account list -o table
# Shows both tenants' subscriptions

# Switch to a subscription in a specific tenant:
az account set --subscription "Fabrikam Production"

# Run a single command in a different subscription without switching:
az resource list \
  --subscription "Contoso Dev" \
  --resource-group rg-dev \
  -o table

# Check which tenant the current subscription belongs to:
az account show --query "{Sub:name, Tenant:tenantId}" -o table

# Guest access: if you are a guest in a tenant, specify the tenant at login:
az login --tenant "customer-tenant.onmicrosoft.com" --allow-no-subscriptions
# --allow-no-subscriptions: needed if you have no subscriptions but need
# to run Azure AD / Graph commands in that tenant

Using a certificate instead of a client secret is more secure because the private key never leaves the machine that holds it. Certificates can have shorter lifespans and are harder to accidentally expose in logs than plaintext secrets.

# Generate a self-signed certificate (valid 1 year):
openssl req -x509 -newkey rsa:4096 \
  -keyout sp-cert.key \
  -out sp-cert.crt \
  -days 365 \
  -nodes \
  -subj "/CN=sp-pipeline-cert"

# Combine into PEM for az login:
cat sp-cert.crt sp-cert.key > sp-cert.pem

# Upload the certificate's public key to the app registration:
APP_ID="aaaaaaaa-0000-0000-0000-000000000000"
az ad app credential reset \
  --id "$APP_ID" \
  --cert "@sp-cert.crt" \
  --append

# Authenticate using the certificate:
az login \
  --service-principal \
  --username "$APP_ID" \
  --certificate sp-cert.pem \
  --tenant "$TENANT_ID"

# Verify:
az account show -o table

# Store the cert in Azure Key Vault for CI/CD retrieval:
az keyvault certificate import \
  --vault-name kv-pipeline \
  --name sp-pipeline-cert \
  --file sp-cert.pfx   # convert PEM to PFX first with openssl pkcs12
Authentication in Azure Automation and Functions
Azure Automation runbooks and Azure Functions can authenticate to the CLI using managed identities, making them credential-free by design.

# In an Azure Automation runbook (PowerShell):
# Enable managed identity on the Automation Account first:
# Automation Account > Identity > System assigned > On

# Then in the runbook:
# Connect-AzAccount -Identity

# Equivalent using Azure CLI in a Bash runbook:
az login --identity

# Run commands against the subscription:
az vm list --resource-group rg-prod -o table

# In Azure Functions (Python):
# Set the AZURE_CLIENT_ID, AZURE_TENANT_ID env vars, or use system MSI
# The Azure SDK's DefaultAzureCredential handles this automatically

# Grant a Function App's managed identity the Reader role on a resource group:
FUNC_PRINCIPAL=$(az functionapp identity show \
  --name func-my-app \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --query principalId -o tsv)

az role assignment create \
  --role Reader \
  --assignee-object-id "$FUNC_PRINCIPAL" \
  --assignee-principal-type ServicePrincipal \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/rg-prod"

# Now the Function App can call Azure CLI or SDK without credentials:
# az login --identity is called inside the Function's code
Azure AD records all sign-in events, including CLI logins. Use the Azure AD sign-in logs to audit who authenticated when and from where.

# List your own recent sign-ins via the CLI (requires Microsoft Graph access):
az rest \
  --method GET \
  --uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/me/authentication/methods" \
  --headers "Content-Type=application/json"

# Admins: list sign-in activity for a specific service principal:
SP_OBJECT_ID=$(az ad sp show --id "$APP_ID" --query id -o tsv)
az rest \
  --method GET \
  --uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/auditLogs/signIns?\$filter=servicePrincipalId eq '$SP_OBJECT_ID'&\$top=10" \
  --query "value[].{Time:createdDateTime, IP:ipAddress, Status:status.errorCode}" \
  -o table

# List all service principal credentials and their expiry dates:
# (useful for proactive secret rotation)
az ad app list --all --query "[].{App:displayName, AppId:appId}" -o table | head -20

# For a single app, list credential expiry:
az ad app credential list \
  --id "$APP_ID" \
  --query "[].{Name:displayName, End:endDateTime}" \
  -o table

The Azure CLI supports four authentication methods. Use 'az login' for interactive developer sessions — it opens a browser, supports MFA, and caches the token locally. Use a service principal with a client secret or certificate for CI/CD pipelines on infrastructure that does not support managed identity; rotate secrets on a schedule. Use managed identity (az login --identity) for workloads running inside Azure — no credentials to manage. Use workload identity federation for GitHub Actions and similar CI/CD platforms — it eliminates client secrets entirely by trusting the platform's own OIDC tokens. In all non-interactive scenarios, apply the principle of least privilege: grant the service principal or managed identity only the specific RBAC role it needs on the specific scope it needs.
